t1.title: What is Backend Engineering?
t1.what: Backend engineering is the discipline of building server-side systems that power applications — APIs, databases, authentication, business logic, and infrastructure.
t1.why: Every product needs a reliable, scalable backend. Frontend is the face; backend is the brain and backbone. Without it, no data persists, no logic executes, no system scales.
t1.when: Whenever you build anything beyond a static page: user accounts, data storage, real-time features, payments, AI integrations, or multi-service architectures.
t1.how: A client sends an HTTP request → the server routes it → middleware processes auth/validation → a handler executes business logic → the database is queried → a response is returned.
t1.ecosystem: Node.js, Python (Django/Flask/FastAPI), Go, Java (Spring Boot), Rust, PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ, Kafka, Docker, Kubernetes, AWS/GCP/Azure.
t1.realWorld: Netflix processes 2+ billion API requests daily. Stripe's backend handles payment processing across 46 countries. Uber's backend orchestrates 25M+ rides daily.
advantages: Full control over data and business logic
Security boundaries at the server level
t1.interviewTip: When asked 'What does a backend engineer do?', go beyond 'write APIs.' Talk about system reliability, data integrity, scalability patterns, and operational excellence.
t1.resumeBullet: Designed and implemented RESTful backend services handling 10K+ requests/sec with <50ms p99 latency using Node.js and PostgreSQL.

lcet1.title: What is Low-Code Engineering?
lcet1.what: Low-code engineering is the discipline of building software applications using visual development platforms that minimize the need for manual hand-coding. It combines pre-built components, visual models, and automated workflows.
lcet1.why: It significantly reduces time-to-market, addresses the shortage of specialized skills, and allows for rapid prototyping and deployment of enterprise-grade applications.
lcet1.when: Ideal for business process automation, internal tools, customer portals, and when speed of delivery is more critical than low-level system control.
lcet1.how: Developers use drag-and-drop interfaces to design UIs, define data models, and orchestrate logic via visual flows, adding custom code only for complex integrations or unique logic.
lcet1.ecosystem: OutSystems, Mendix, Microsoft Power Platform, Appian, ServiceNow, Salesforce Lightning, APIs, connectors, and cloud services.
lcet1.realWorld: A global logistics firm used a low-code platform to build a comprehensive freight tracking system in just 4 weeks, a project previously estimated at 6 months.
lcet1.interviewTip: Emphasize how low-code is a strategic choice for business agility, not a replacement for 'real' coding. Highlight your ability to extend low-code with custom logic when needed.
lcet1.resumeBullet: Accelerated application delivery by 75% using low-code platforms for a fleet management system, integrating with legacy SOAP APIs via custom connectors.

t2.title: HTTP, REST & API Design
t2.what: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications using HTTP methods to perform CRUD operations.
t2.why: REST is the lingua franca of web APIs. Understanding it deeply separates junior developers from engineers who can design systems that last.
t2.when: Whenever you need machine-to-machine or client-server communication. REST is ideal for stateless, cacheable, resource-oriented interactions.
t2.how: Resources are identified by URIs. HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) map to operations. Status codes communicate outcomes.
t2.ecosystem: OpenAPI/Swagger, Postman, cURL, Express.js, FastAPI, Spring Boot, API Gateway (AWS/GCP), rate limiters, API versioning strategies.
t2.realWorld: GitHub's REST API serves millions of developers daily. Stripe's API is considered the gold standard.
disadvantages: Can become chatty for complex operations
t2.interviewTip: Know the difference between PUT and PATCH. Understand idempotency. Be able to design a clean API for any domain in under 5 minutes.
t2.resumeBullet: Architected RESTful API layer with OpenAPI spec, versioning strategy, and rate limiting, reducing integration time by 60%.

t3.title: SQL & Relational Databases
t3.what: Relational databases organize data into tables with defined schemas, relationships (via foreign keys), and use SQL for querying. They guarantee ACID properties.
t3.why: Most business data is inherently relational. Understanding SQL deeply is non-negotiable for any backend engineer.
t3.when: When you need strong consistency, complex queries with joins, transactions, or well-structured data with clear relationships.
t3.how: Data is stored in rows within tables. Indexes speed up reads. Query planners optimize execution. Transactions ensure atomicity.
t3.ecosystem: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server. ORMs: Prisma, TypeORM, Sequelize, SQLAlchemy, Hibernate.
t3.realWorld: Instagram runs on PostgreSQL and handles billions of rows. Banks rely on ACID-compliant databases for transactions.
disadvantages: Can be slower for simple key-value lookups
t3.interviewTip: Be ready to write complex SQL queries on the spot. Know the difference between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, and CROSS JOIN. Understand indexing trade-offs.
t3.resumeBullet: Optimized PostgreSQL queries reducing p95 latency from 800ms to 45ms through strategic indexing and query plan analysis.
